coredata.dc:title: Exploration of biomass-derived carbon dots based on chestnut shell for the sensitive detection of phosphate and tetracycline hydrochloride
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Zirui
Wang, Haowei
Tang, Xiaodan
Mou, Mengshi
Yu, Hongmei
Wang, Huiyong
coredata.dc:description: Phosphate pollution leads to the deterioration of water quality, posing a serious threat to human health. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), a class of broad-spectrum bacteriostatic agents, has garnered attention due to its extensive use and potential toxicity. Therefore, developing a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of phosphates and TC is of significant importance. Herein, to enhance the conversion and utilization of high-value biomass waste, biomass-derived carbon dots (LZ-NCDs) emitting green fluorescence with a quantum yield of 44 % were synthesized in a one-step hydrothermal process using chestnut shell biomass waste as a carbon source and nitrogen doping technology. Based on the dynamic quenching mechanism, a highly sensitive method for effectively identifying PO4 3- using LZ-NCDs fluorescence probe was constructed, with a linear range of 0.1–10 µmol/L and a detection limit of 43.0 nmol/L. A quenched fluorescent probe, LZ-NCDs for the determination of TC, was fabricated through the synergistic effects of inner filter effect and static quenching, exhibiting a linear range from 0.05 to 10 µmol/L with a detection limit of 16.8 nmol/L. The successful determination of PO4 3- and TC in actual samples was achieved. The two different quenching mechanisms indicate that LZ-NCDs are expected to become potential sensing materials for the real-time monitoring of PO4 3- and TC in organisms and food, which is very important for our health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Chestnut shell
Phosphate
Tetracycline hydrochloride
Fluorescence analysis
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of chestnut Shell-Based fluorescent CDs (LZ-NCDs)
“turn-off” LZ-NCDs fluorescent probe for the identification of PO4 3- and TC
Characterization and Spectral performance of LZ-NCDs
Effective recognition of PO4 3- by “turn off”LZ-NCDs fluorescent probe
“Turn off” recognition of TC based on LZ-NCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM (inset: particle size distribution), (B) HRTEM, (C) XRD and FT-IR images of LZ-NCDs.
(A) XPS full spectrum of LZ-NCDs; High-resolution spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D) of LZ-NCDs.

(A) UV, maximum excitation and emission spectra of LZ-NCDs. Inset: photographs of LZ-NCDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (B) color coordinate diagram of LZ-NCDs; (C) fluorescence emission spectra of LZ-NCDs under different excitation wavelengths.

The effect of concentration of NaCl(A), pH of PBS buffer solution(B), oxidation time(C), oxidation concentration(D), continuous irradiation time with a 365 nm UV-lamp (E) and xenon lamp (F) on the fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs.

F/F0 of LZ-NCDs to different metal ions (A) and anions(B); (C) interference of PO4 3- quenching by other co-existing ions; (D) effect of incubation time with PO4 3- on fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs (the illustration is a spectrogram).

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of LZ-NCDs with various concentrations of PO4 3- (0.1–110 μmol/L). Inset: images of LZ-NCDs with various concentrations of PO4 3- (0, 3, 10, 20, 40, 60, 70, 90 and 100 µmol/L) under UV lamp; (B) relationship between F0/F with the full concentration of PO4 3-. Inset: linear fitting graph.

(A) Overlapping graph of the UV absorption of PO4 3-, the excitation and emission spectra of LZ-NCDs; (B) effect of different concentrations of PO4 3- on the UV absorption spectrum of LZ-NCDs; (C) fitting plots of F0/F versus various concentrations of PO4 3- at different temperatures; (D) fluorescence decay curves of LZ-NCDs after addition of 0 µmol/L, 30 µmol/L and 50 µmol/L PO4 3-.

(A) The effect of drugs on the fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs; (B) interference diagram of co-existing drug on LZ-NCDs-TC system; (C) effect of incubation time of LZ-NCDs and TC on fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs (The illustration is a spectrogram).

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of LZ-NCDs with various concentrations of TC (0.05–110 μmol/L). Inset: images under UV lamp; (B) relationship between F0/F with the full concentration of TC. Inset: linear relationship between F0/F with the concentration of TC.

(A) The overlap between the UV spectra of TC and the excitation and emission spectra of LZ-NCDs; (B) the influence of different concentrations of TC on the UV spectra of LZ-NCDs; (C) fitting graphs of Stern-Volmer equation at different temperatures; (d) fluorescence decay curves of LZ-NCDs in the absence and presence of 20 μmol/L and 50 μmol/L TC.
The ftir spectra of lz-ncds before and after the addition of tc.
XPS full spectrum of LZ-NCDs with TC; High-resolution spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D) of LZ-NCDs.
(A) The CV curve of LZ-NCDs. Inset: E g calculated by UV spectrum of LZ-NCDs; (B) CV curve of TC (Inset: E g calculated by UV spectrum of TC); (C) Electron transfer mechanism of LZ-NCDs and TC.
(A) Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal equation fitting diagram of 1/(F0-F) to 1/c TC at different temperatures; (B) Linear fitting diagram of van’t Hoff equation.
Preparation of LZ-NCDs and recognition mechanism of PO43- and TC.
The mechanism schematic of TC binding by LZ-NCDs.
Comparison of different analytical methods for the detection of PO4 3-.
Double-exponential fitting of LZ-CDs and PO4 3- decay curves.
Application of LZ-NCDs fluorescent probe in practical samples.
Comparison of the fluorescence analytical methods for TC detection.
Fluorescence lifetime decay of the LZ-CDs with different concentrations of TC.
Binding constants and thermodynamic parameters of LZ-NCDs and TC.
Application of LZ-NCDs for the detection of TC in actual sample (n = 3, 95 % confidence).

head.ce:title: Exploration of biomass-derived carbon dots based on chestnut shell for the sensitive detection of phosphate and tetracycline hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Liaoning, Anshan 114051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Liaoning, Anshan 114051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphate pollution leads to the deterioration of water quality, posing a serious threat to human health. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), a class of broad-spectrum bacteriostatic agents, has garnered attention due to its extensive use and potential toxicity. Therefore, developing a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of phosphates and TC is of significant importance. Herein, to enhance the conversion and utilization of high-value biomass waste, biomass-derived carbon dots (LZ-NCDs) emitting green fluorescence with a quantum yield of 44 % were synthesized in a one-step hydrothermal process using chestnut shell biomass waste as a carbon source and nitrogen doping technology. Based on the dynamic quenching mechanism, a highly sensitive method for effectively identifying PO4 3- using LZ-NCDs fluorescence probe was constructed, with a linear range of 0.1–10 µmol/L and a detection limit of 43.0 nmol/L. A quenched fluorescent probe, LZ-NCDs for the determination of TC, was fabricated through the synergistic effects of inner filter effect and static quenching, exhibiting a linear range from 0.05 to 10 µmol/L with a detection limit of 16.8 nmol/L. The successful determination of PO4 3- and TC in actual samples was achieved. The two different quenching mechanisms indicate that LZ-NCDs are expected to become potential sensing materials for the real-time monitoring of PO4 3- and TC in organisms and food, which is very important for our health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorus, existing widely in the natural environment as phosphate, is a crucial biological anion essential for the growth of most organisms. It is a key nutrient for nucleic acids, proteins, and molecular energy carriers. Phosphorus also participates in many important physiological and pathological processes [1,2]. Phosphate deficiency can lead to hypophosphatemia, central nervous system disorders, heart function impairment, and even respiratory failure [3]. Furthermore, phosphate is an indispensable nutrient element for aquatic microorganisms that can promote the growth of aquatic plants and improve water quality. However, excessive discharge of organic phosphorus pesticides and phosphate fertilizers from industrial and agricultural activities has led to rampant proliferation of algae and bacteria, causing eutrophication and death of aquatic organisms. This not only affects the safety of human drinking water but also results in significant ecological harm [4,5]. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic produced by Streptomyces [6], exhibiting excellent inhibitory effects on Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, as well as on Chlamydiae, Rickettsiae, and Mycoplasma, which has been extensively utilized in the treatment of diseases in both humans and animals [7]. Additionally, TC, due to its low cost, good oral absorption, and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, is widely used in animal husbandry for the prevention and treatment of bacterial infections [8]. TC is included in the World Health Organization's (WHO) model list of essential medicines, representing one of the most necessary drugs in public health systems [9]. However, the excessive and continuous misuse of TC in recent years has led to residues in animal-derived food products, such as milk, meat, and egg, and has also caused pollution in aquatic environments [10]. Long-term consumption of food containing residual antibiotics may lead to an increase in drug-resistant bacteria, causing allergic reactions, gastrointestinal disorders, and hepatotoxicity [11]. Therefore, the development of a sensitive and rapid method for detecting trace amounts of phosphate ion (PO4 3-) and TC is of significant importance for environmental management and human health.

At present, many analytical methods including electrochemistry [12,14], enzyme biosensor [13], colorimetry [15], spectrophotometry [16], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [17] have been applied to quantitative determination of TC in samples. Although these methods offer favorable selectivity and adequate sensitivity, they have certain limitations, including cumbersome operational procedures, time-consuming processes and complex sample pretreatment, which render them impractical for large-scale routine environmental monitoring. In comparison with these methods, fluorescence analysis technique has garnered substantial interest among researchers due to its simplicity, convenience, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity. Various types of fluorescent probes have been successively developed for the detection of phosphate and TC, including carbon dots (CDs) [10,18,21,22,23,24], graphene quantum dots（QDs） [9,19], metal–organic frameworks [1,20,25,26]. As a new member of the carbon nanomaterials family, carbon point (CDs) has good application potential in chemical and biological sensing [7,10,21,22,23,24], bioimaging [18] and drug delivery [27] due to its advantages of low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high light stability, tunable fluorescence (FL) emission and environmental friendliness. Zou et al. [18] developed a composite probe (CDs-Fe3+) for the fluorescent determination of phosphate in real water samples and cells, with a detection limit of 0.25 µmol/L within the concentration range of 0.4 to 22 µmol/L. Wu et al. [20] prepared a ratiometric fluorescent probe (CDs@ZIF-90) for the detection of PO4 3-, featuring a linear range from 1.0 to 50 µmol/L and a detection limit of 0.23 µmol/L, which was successfully applied to assess the content of PO4 3− in actual water samples. Based on the internal filtering effect and static quenching, Zhou [23] prepared self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from mulberry leaves using a facile one-step hydrothermal method, constructing a rapid, naked eye, double-readout assay for tetracycline detection with a detection limit of 158 nmol/L. Li [25] developed a novel sensing platform (COF@Eu3+) for tetracycline determination. The red fluorescence of Eu3+ was enhanced by tetracycline with green fluorescence of COF as a reference. The resulting ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrates a linear range of 0.1–20 µmol/L for tetracycline, with a detection limit of 30 nmol/L. In summary, the current fluorescence analysis methods for the detection of phosphate and TC have relatively detection limits, which limit their application in trace analysis of actual samples. Amidst the growing depletion of the earth's resources and the severe destruction of the ecological environment, the development of renewable resources and the maintenance of the coordination and sustainable development of resources and environment are important research directions of current environmental protection. Biomass resources, characterized by their abundance, renewability, environmental friendliness, low cost of raw materials, and potential for high-value utilization, can serve as effective alternatives to traditional chemical reagent and have become a focus of widespread attention among researchers. Biomass waste contains a wealth of ash, proteins, and lignin, in which nitrogen and sulfur elements from lignin may contribute to the heteroatom doping of CDs, improving the optical and electronic properties of CDs, and enhancing their fluorescence quantum yield (QY). CDs derived from biomass may inherit the porous characteristics of biomass, which helps to increase the specific surface area of CDs, enhancing their potential applications in catalysis, adsorption, and sensors. Currently, numerous biomass waste have been synthesized into fluorescent carbon dots, such as grape peels [28], laurel leaves [29], mango peel [30], Papaya seeds [31], tea seed husk [32], olive leaves [33] and other biomass wastes [23,34,35,8]. However, CDs derived from biomass often suffer from weak fluorescence intensity. Introducing heteroatoms into the carbon framework can effectively modulate the density of states of CDs, thus enhancing their fluorescence intensity [18,32]. Wang [32] developed a fluorescence visual sensing method for glyphosine using tea seed husk powder and melamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, and the quantum yield was 15.58 %. Zhao [33] developed negatively charged, tunable multicolor nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) using mango peel as a source material, which exhibited good biocompatibility and photostability, making them suitable for multicolor cell labeling in vitro. Therefore, the development of biomass-based CDs with high quantum yield fluorescence probes represents a topic that is both realistic and challenging.

Herein, using chestnut shell waste as a carbon source and meta-phenylenediamine (MPD) as a dopant, green synthesis of green-light-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (LZ-NCDs) was achieved in a one-step hydrothermal process without the addition of any organic solvents (Scheme 1 ). The optical properties, surface functional groups, and morphology of CDs were analyzed using techniques such as fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Based on the dynamic quenching mechanism, a “turn off” type fluorescence detection method for sensitively identifying PO4 3- with LZ-NCDs fluorescence probe was constructed. Additionally, effective quenching of TC by LZ-NCDs was achieved through inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism. The LZ-NCDs probe has been successfully applied for the quantitative analysis of PO4 3- and TC in biological and environmental water samples. Compared to previously reported analytical methods, the constructed fluorescence probe demonstrates superior photoluminescent properties, holding broad prospects in the fields of disease diagnostics, biosensing, and water quality monitoring.

The morphology and lattice structure were characterized by JEM-ARM2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan) and D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruck, Germany), respectively. Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edingburgh, UK) and U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed on the Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded using Nicolet iS 10 spectrometers (Thermo fisher, USA). Fluorescence lifetime was obtained on FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, Japan). The cyclic voltammetry curve was analyzed by CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China).

The chestnuts were purchased from a local supermarket, peeled after washing, and dried for later use. Sodium phosphate and quinine sulfate were purchased from Guoyao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shenyang, China). MPD, TC, amino acids, and other drugs were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical grade. Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1) was used for the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chestnut Shell-Based fluorescent CDs (LZ-NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Chestnut shell powder (1.0 g) and MPD (1.0 g) were dissolved completely in 15 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 5 min. Then, the solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was filtered and centrifuged. The supernatant was dialyzed for 48 h (Mw = 500 Da), and the purified LZ-NCDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying, which was then stored in a refrigerator for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “turn-off” LZ-NCDs fluorescent probe for the identification of PO4 3- and TC
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the prepared LZ-NCDs for the detection of PO4 3- and TC was evaluated utilizing a fluorescence titration method. In a cuvette, 2 mL of LZ-NCDs solution (1 mg/mL) was introduced, followed by the incremental addition of varying volumes of 0.01 mol/L PO4 3- stock solution or 0.005 mol/L TC stock solution using a micropipette, thereby establishing various concentrations of LZ-NCDs-PO4 3- (LZ-NCDs-TC) reaction systems. Following incubation at room temperature with mixing for 0.5 min, the fluorescence spectrum at 532 nm was recorded using an excitation wavelength of 430 nm.

Environmental water samples were collected from laboratory tap water, urine samples were obtained from healthy adults, and milk and honey were purchased from local supermarkets. All actual samples were filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The spiked recovery method was employed to verify the accuracy and feasibility of LZ-NCDs as fluorescent probes for detecting the content of PO4 3- and TC in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and Spectral performance of LZ-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A depicts the TEM image of LZ-NCDs, which reveals that LZ-NCDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed. Utilizing Image J software, a statistical analysis was conducted on 100 randomly selected nanoparticles, revealing an average particle size of 4.52 ± 0.87 nm for LZ-NCDs. Fig. 1 B presents the HRTEM spectrum of LZ-NCDs, illustrating clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of approximately 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite carbon (sp2), indicating that LZ-NCDs possess a graphitic-like structure [19]. Fig. 1C characterizes the microstructure of NL-CDs using XRD patterns. The broad diffraction peak of LZ-NCDs appears at 21.02°, which aligns with the interplanar spacing (d = 0.34 nm) of the (002) plane of graphitic carbon materials, indicating that LZ-NCDs possess a graphitic structure [7,36]. The infrared spectrum of LZ-NCDs is shown in Fig. 1D. The absorption peaks at 3350 cm−1 and 3219 cm−1 correspond to the typical O-H and N-H stretching vibration, respectively [7,19,22]. The peak at 2912 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H stretching vibration [24,28,32], while the absorption peak at 1635 cm−1 is associated with the C=O stretching vibration [22,33]. Additionally, the peaks at 1442 cm−1 and 1053 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O, respectively [19,22,24]. These characteristic peaks indicate that LZ-NCDs surface contains abundant hydrophilic groups such as –NH2, –COOH and –OH.

Fig. 2 A presents the XPS spectrum of LZ-NCDs, indicating three characteristic peaks located at 285.27 eV, 399.81 eV, and 532.55 eV, representing C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The element proportions of LZ-CDs were 69.2 % of carbon, 5.33 % of nitrogen and 25.47 % of oxygen. Fig. 2B displays the high-resolution C1s spectrum of LZ-NCDs, which can be deconvoluted into four distinctive peaks situated at 283.8 eV, 284.37 eV, 285.75 eV, and 287.84 eV, attributed to C-C/C=C, C-N, C-O/C-O-C, and C=O groups, respectively [24,37]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2C) reveals characteristic peaks at 398.79 eV and 399.68 eV, which correspond to the C-N and N-H groups, respectively [32]. Simultaneously, the O1s spectrum of LZ-NCDs (Fig. 2 D) exhibits two peaks located at 531.08 eV and 532.24 eV, representing C=O and C-OH/C-O-C groups respectively [7,19]. The results from the XPS spectra indicate that nitrogen atoms have been successfully doped onto the surface of LZ-NCDs, with the surface coated with oxygen-containing functional groups.

Fig. 3 A reveals that the maximum excitation and emission spectra of LZ-NCDs are located at 430 nm and 532 nm, respectively, exhibiting good symmetry. The UV spectrum displays an absorption peaks at 273 nm, attributed to π-π* electronic transition of C=C bonds. The absorption peak at 388 nm is likely caused by n-π* transition of C=O bonds [20,24].

The illustration shows that under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the LZ-NCDs aqueous solution exhibits a bright green color (right). The color coordinate diagram of LZ-NCDs (Fig. 3B) also confirms that the luminescence region of LZ-NCDs is green. Further investigation reveals that as the excitation wavelength increases from 390 nm to 480 nm, the fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs initially increases and then decreases, but the fluorescence emission peak position does not change significantly, indicating that LZ-NCDs do not exhibit excitation dependency (Fig. 3C). Using the slope comparison method [7,28], the fluorescence quantum yield of LZ-NCDs was calculated to be 44.0 %, which is significantly higher than that of undoped biomass carbon dots [34,35,38].

The fluorescence stability of carbon dots is essential for the application in chemical sensing. Fig. 4 A demonstrates that in a high ionic strength environment of 4 mol/L, the fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs only experiences a slight decrease, indicating that LZ-NCDs can stably exist in high ionic strength environments. Fig. 4B illustrates that LZ-NCDs exhibit a strong fluorescence response in the PBS buffer system at pH = 7. The neutral environment is highly beneficial for the practical applications of LZ-NCDs in bioimaging or sensing. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 4C and 4D, the fluorescence of LZ-NCDs remains highly stable after continuous oxidation in a 300 µmol/L H2O2 solution for 120 min and varying H2O2 concentrations from 100 µmol/L to 500 µmol/L. Additionally, The fluorescence intensity of the LZ-NCDs solution only slightly decreases after prolonged irradiation under a UV lamp (25 μw cm−2) and a xenon lamp (50 μw cm−2) (Fig. 4E and 4F), indicating that LZ-NCDs possess good resistance to photobleaching. The outstanding fluorescence stability suggests that the prepared LZ-NCDs fluorescent probe has superior application potential in the analysis of complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effective recognition of PO4 3- by “turn off”LZ-NCDs fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5B, only the addition of PO4 3- to the LZ-NCDs solution can effectively quench the fluorescence of LZ-NCDs. Compared to other metals and anions, LZ-NCDs exhibit higher selectivity for PO4 3-. Interference experiments (Fig. 5C) indicate that coexisting substances do not affect the quenching of LZ-NCDs fluorescence by PO4 3-. This suggests that LZ-NCDs, as a probe, have a high degree of recognition and specificity for PO4 3-. Fig. 5D demonstrates that the fluorescence emission intensity is quenched to its maximum at 30 s, indicating that LZ-NCDs have a rapid response to PO4 3-.

To investigate the sensitive response of LZ-NCDs to PO4 3-, the effect of different concentrations of PO4 3- (0.1–110 μmol/L) on the fluorescence spectrum was examined (Fig. 6 A). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs at 532 nm gradually decreased with increasing PO4 3-concentration, indicating that LZ-NCDs are highly sensitive to the concentration of PO4 3-. The inset of Fig. 6 A demonstrates that as the concentration of PO4 3- increases, the bright green fluorescence of LZ-NCDs under UV light gradually diminishes, consistent with the attenuation observed in the fluorescence spectrum. Fig. 6B presents the calibration plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of LZ-NCDs against the concentration of PO4 3-. It is observed that across the entire concentration range, a linear fit is not feasible. However, good linear relationships was achieved within the ranges of 0.1–10 μmol/L and 20–90 μmol/L. Within the scope of 0.1–10 μmol/L, the linear equation is F0/F = 0.072[c PO4 3-: μmol/L] + 1.042 (R2 = 0.9979). In the range of 20–90 μmol/L, the equation is F0/F = 0.024[c PO4 3-: μmol/L] + 1.413 (R2 = 0.9995). Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of PO4 3-, respectively. According to LOD = 3σ/K sv, the detection limit is calculated to be 43.0 nmol/L in the range of 0.1–10 μ mol/L. Compared with other detection methods (Table 1 ), the fluorescence probe based on LZ-NCDs can compete with existing PO4 3- sensors. This probe has the advantages of simple preparation, environmental friendliness, and superior sensitivity, which can achieve real-time, rapid, and non-labeled detection of PO4 3- in practical samples.

The oxygen anions in the phosphate group have strong nucleophilic characteristics and can undergo hydrogen bonding with functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl on the surface of LZ-NCDs, leading to fluorescence quenching [39]. Therefore, further investigation was conducted on the quenching mechanism of PO4 3- on LZ-NCDs. Fig. 7 A indicates that PO4 3- exhibits no significant absorption peak between 200 nm and 300 nm, and shows no overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of LZ-NCDs, thereby ruling out the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [24,28,40]. Zeta potential measurements were conducted on LZ-CDs and LZ-CDs with PO4 3- separately, with LZ-CDs exhibiting a potential value of −2.16 mV and LZ-CDs with PO4 3- showing a potential value of −8.89 mV. The negative charges on the surface of LZ-CDs and the phosphate ions create a repulsive force between them, affecting the proximity of the quencher to the fluorophore. The distance between the two may exceed 10 nm, which is not conducive to the occurrence of the FRET mechanism. Because FRET requires the distance between the fluorophore and the quencher to be within a certain range (less than 10 nm) for efficient energy transfer to occur [41]. The UV spectrum of varying concentrations of PO4 3- in the LZ-NCDs solution (Fig. 7B) showed that the UV absorption peak did not change significantly with the increase of PO4 3- concentration, indicating that PO4 3- and LZ-NCDs did not form a non-fluorescent ground-state complex. This suggests that the fluorescence quenching type of NCDs by PO4 3- may be due to dynamic quenching. According to the Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = 1 + K sv[Q] [42], the fitting curves of F0/F versus the concentration of PO4 3- (Fig. 7 C) were plotted at different temperatures. Here, K sv is the quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher PO4 3-. It was observed that as the temperature increased, the quenching constant K sv gradually increased. This indicates that the rise in temperature accelerates molecular motion and collision frequency, resulting in an increase of the quenching constant from 2.27 × 104 L/mol to 4.66 × 104 L/mol. The fluorescence lifetime of LZ-NCDs was analyzed using a double-exponential fluorescence decay curve [43] (Fig. 7 D), the relevant data are summarized in Table 2 . Without the addition of PO4 3-, the fluorescence lifetime was 5.53 ns. Upon the addition of 30 μmol/L and 50 μmol/L PO4 3-, the fluorescence lifetimes were 4.24 ns and 3.95 ns, respectively. The significant change in fluorescence lifetime further confirms that the quenching of LZ-NCDs by PO4 3- is due to dynamic quenching.

To assess the potential of LZ-NCDs as fluorescent probes for detecting PO4 3- in real samples, spiking recovery experiments were conducted in lake water, urine, and serum (Table 3 ). The fluorescent probe developed with LZ-NCDs successfully detected PO4 3- in spiked samples, with recovery rates ranging from 95.5 % to 104.8 %. These results indicate that the established analytical method is accurate and feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn off” recognition of TC based on LZ-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: When various drugs were introduced to the LZ-NCDs solution, only TC significantly quenched the fluorescence of LZ-NCDs (Fig. 8 A). The degree of quenching with other drugs present was consistent with that observed when TC was added alone (Fig. 8B), indicating that LZ-NCDs exhibit excellent selectivity and anti-interference capabilities for recognition of TC. Fig. 8C demonstrates that TC can quench the fluorescence of LZ-NCDs to its maximum extent within 30 s, and this remains unchanged.

When different concentrations (0.05–110 μmol/L) of TC were sequentially added to the LZ-NCDs solution (Fig. 9 A), the fluorescence intensity of LZ-NCDs at 532 nm gradually decreased with the increase of TC concentration, indicating that LZ-NCDs are highly sensitive to the concentration of TC. The illustration shows that the bright green fluorescence under the UV lamp gradually diminishes as the concentration of TC increases, demonstrating the potential for visual monitoring of TC content. Fig. 9 B is a curve fitting plot of F0/F versus the concentration of TC. It is noteworthy that in the entire range, la linear relationship cannot be presented. It is only linear within the concentration range of 0.05–10 μmol/L with F0/F = 0.185[c TC:μmol/L] + 1.076 and R2 = 0.9997. The detection limit of LZ-NCDs for TC is calculated to be 16.8 nmol/L. Compared to other methods for detecting TC (Table 4 ), LZ-NCDs synthesized from kitchen waste, such as chestnut shells, are environmentally friendly, low-cost, and offer a low detection limit, making them more promising for the detection of TC than the reported sensing systems.

Fig. 10 elaborates on the fluorescence quenching mechanism between TC and LZ-NCDs. TC exhibits a broad absorption peak in the wavelength range of 250–450 nm, which partially overlaps with the excitation spectrum of LZ-NCDs (Fig. 10 A), leading to the quenching of LZ-NCDs due to partial shading of the excitation light. This phenomenon is consistent with the conditions for internal filtration effect. There is no overlap with the emission spectra of LZ-NCDs, which rules out the possibility of FRET mechanism. Simultaneously, the Zeta potentials of LZ-CDs and TC were determined to be −2.16 mV and −6.95 mV, respectively. Both are negatively charged, indicating that repulsive forces generate, which might cause the distance between LZ-CDss and TC to be greater than 10 nm, eliminating the FRET quenching mechanism. As depicted in Fig. 10 B, with the increase of TC concentration, the absorption peak of LZ-NCDs at 290 nm gradually shifts to the blue, indicating that TC forms a non-luminescent ground-state complex with LZ-NCDs, leading to changes in the UV absorption peak, which is likely due to static quenching causing fluorescence decay. The variation of K sv in the LZ-NCDs-TC system at different temperatures (303 K, 313 K and 323 K) was plotted from Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. 10 C). It was observed that K SV decreased from 21.07 × 104 L/mol to 8.67 × 104L / mol as temperature increased, suggesting that the complex formed between TC and LZ-NCDs becomes unstable, gradually dissociating, which leads to a decrease in Ksv. This further confirms that the quenching type of TC on LZ-NCDs is static quenching. This further confirms that the quenching type of TC on LZ-NCDs is static quenching. The lifetimes of LZ-CDs in the absence and presence of TC were measured and the decay spectra fitted with bi-exponential curve are displayed in Fig. 10D. The relevant data are summarized in Table 5 . The fluorescence lifetimes of LZ-NCDs were 5.53 ns without the addition of TC, and 5.49 ns and 5.45 ns with the addition of 20 μmol/L and 50 μmol/L TC, respectively. The lack of significant change in fluorescence lifetime is consistent with the static quenching mechanism, ruling out the occurrence of FRET between LZ-NCDs and TC.

By comparing the FTIR spectra of LZ-NCDs before and after the addition of TC (Fig. 11 ), it was observed that the absorption peaks intensity corresponding to functional groups (hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl) in the FTIR spectra changed, which suggests that TC may have formed hydrogen bonds with amino or hydroxyl groups on the surface of LZ-NCDs, leading to the formation of a stable complex. These findings provide evidence that static quenching is responsible for the reduction in fluorescence of LZ-NCDs.

Meanwhile, the XPS spectra of LZ-NCDs with TC was analyzed (Fig. 12 ). In the full XPS spectrum, three characteristic peaks centered at 285.89 eV 398.63 eV, and 531.59 eV were observed, which attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s with atomic ratios of 67.88 %, 7.84 %, and 24.28 %, respectively. It was observed that the peak shapes of C 1 s and N 1 s in LZ-CDs changed before (Fig. 2) and after adding TC (Fig. 12), and the peak position also shifted. This suggests that TC may have formed complexes with specific elements on the surface of LZ-NCDs, further confirming that the quenching of LZ-NCDs is due to static quenching mechanism.

To further verify whether the fluorescence quenching is caused by the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, the electrochemical properties of LZ-NCDs were characterized using cyclic voltammetry. Using the empirical formula (1) and (2) [40,44], the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of LZ-NCDs and TC were calculated. Here, E red represents the reduction potential and E g represents the bandgap width.
(1)
ELUMO = - e ( E r e d + 4.4 )
(2)
EHOMO = E L U M O - E g

From Fig. 13 A, it is known that the E red of LZ-NCDs is −0.56 eV, and the E LUMO of LZ-NCDs is calculated to be −3.84 eV. The bandgap width E g is calculated to be 3.01 eV using the UV spectrum of LZ-NCDs, and the E HOMO of LZ-NCDs is −6.85 eV. According to Fig. 13 B, the E HOMO and E LUMO of TC are −6.81 eV and −3.75 eV, respectively. Fig. 13 C indicates that the excited electrons of LZ-NCDs cannot be transferred from the LUMO level to the LUMO level of TC, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of LZ-NCDs is not produced by PET. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching of LZ-NCDs by TC is caused by the combined effects of IFE and static quenching.

Essentially, the common types of intermolecular forces between a fluorescent probe and its target include hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, hydrophobic, and electrostatic interactions. According to Ross and Subramanian [45], the thermodynamic parameters, enthalpy change (ΔH), entropy change (ΔS), and free energy change (ΔG) are the main evidences for confirming the binding modes between LZ-NCDs and TC.

Utilizing the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot, with 1/(F0-F) on the ordinate and 1/[Q] on the abscissa, fitted curves were plotted at different temperatures (303 K, 313 K, and 323 K) (Fig. 14 A). It was observed that as the temperature rose, the binding constant K LB progressively decreased, from 18.05 × 105 L/μmol to 4.4 × 105 L/μmol, a trend that corresponds with the quenching constant. By employing the Van't Hoff equation (formula 3), combined with (formula 4), the relevant thermodynamic parameters of ΔH, ΔS and ΔG were derived (Fig. 14 B and Table 6 ), where K LB is the binding constant at the corresponding temperature, T is absolute temperature and R is the gas constant (R = 8.314 J/mol K−1). The ΔH and ΔS values for the interaction between LZ-NCDs and TC were −60.15 KJ·mol−1 and −77.91 J·K−1·mol−1, respectively. With ΔG values at varying temperatures all being less than zero, this signifies that the association of LZ-NCDs with TC is a spontaneous exothermic process.
(3)
lnKLB = - Δ H / R T + Δ S / R
(4)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S

Based on the literature [46,47], both ΔH and ΔS values of the interaction between LZ-NCDs and TC were negative, indicating that the binding is primarily governed by hydrogen bond. The rationale might be attributed to the presence of hydroxyl (–OH) and carbonyl (C=O) functional groups on the TC molecule. The hydroxyl groups are capable of forming hydrogen bonds with oxygen or nitrogen atoms on the surface of LZ-NCDs, whereas the carbonyl groups may interact with the electron cloud on this surface through a conjugation effect. Furthermore, the dimethylamino groups within the TC molecule may also engage in interactions with functional groups on the carbon dots surface, thereby further enhancing hydrogen bonds formation. The binding mechanism was simulated, as depicted in Scheme 2 .

To evaluate the feasibility of the synthesized fluorescent probe LZ-NCDs for detecting TC in real samples, a spike recovery method was used to determine the content of TC in lake water, human urine, and tetracycline injection. The results showed a recovery rate ranging from 96.5 % to 103.0 % with RSD values below 5 % (Table 7 ). These findings demonstrate that the fluorescence analysis method established for detecting TC with LZ-NCDs as a fluorescent probe has good accuracy and practical application value.

Considering environmental protection, renewability, and sustainable development, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (LZ-NCDs) emitting bright green light with a quantum yield as high as 44.0 % were prepared using discarded chestnut shells as a carbon source. LZ-NCDs have uniform particle size, good dispersibility, and a surface rich in hydrophilic groups, which endows LZ-NCDs with excellent potential for application in biomedical imaging. Based on the dynamic quenching mechanism, a method for sensitively detecting PO4 3- with a fluorescence quenching sensor using LZ-NCDs was realized, with a linear range of 0.1–10 μmol/L and a detection limit of 43 nmol/L. This method has been successfully applied to the quantitative determination of PO4 3- in environmental water samples and biological samples. Furthermore, leveraging the synergistic effects of IFE and static quenching, a “turn off” fluorescent sensor was developed to identify TC with a linear range of 0.05 to 10.0 μmol/L, achieving a satisfactory detection limit of 16.8 nmol/L, and had successfully achieved the determination of TC in actual samples, with a recovery rate between 96.5 % and 103.0 %. In conclusion, the new biomass fluorescent probe LZ-NCDs, based on two different quenching mechanisms, is expected to be used for real-time monitoring of PO4 3- and TC in environmental water samples and biological bodies, which has important practical significance in clinical diagnosis, pharmaceutical testing, and environmental analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Fund Project (LJKMZ20220654) and 2024 Innovation Training program of University of Science and Technology Liaoning (S202410146093).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploration of room-temperature phosphorescence and new mechanism on carbon dots in a polyacrylamide platform and their applications for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption
